title: Iran Conflict — Strait of Hormuz Instability
description: Active military conflict in the region. The Strait of Hormuz is a chokepoint for ~21 million barrels/day of crude oil and a critical shipping lane. Naval skirmishes and nearby strikes have created a high-risk zone. Jebel Ali (Dubai) suspended operations after nearby strikes. War-risk insurance premiums have tripled to quadrupled for vessels transiting the area. Major carriers including Maersk and Hapag-Lloyd have rerouted around the Cape of Good Hope, adding 1-3 weeks transit time. Asia-to-US spot freight rates have spiked 30-50%.
impacts: Port operations suspended or severely reduced
Container backlog accumulating at transshipment hubs
Major carriers rerouting around Cape of Good Hope
30-50% freight rate increases on Asia-US lanes
Schedule reliability collapsed to 36% on Asia-Middle East trade

title: Drone Incident at Port of Salalah — Terminal Crane Damaged
description: On 28 March 2026, a security incident involving reported drone activity at the Port of Salalah, Oman, damaged a terminal crane and caused a temporary suspension of all operations. Operations gradually resumed on 31 March but throughput remains reduced. This is part of the broader regional instability linked to the Iran conflict.
impacts: Terminal crane damaged — reduced lift capacity

title: Jeddah Arrival Delays — Hormuz Ripple Effects
description: Average arrival delays at Jeddah Islamic Port rose to approximately 2.49 days on a rolling 30-day basis in March-April 2026. Driven by rerouted vessels from the Hormuz conflict zone creating congestion at alternative regional hubs, plus increased security checks at Saudi ports.

title: Panama Supreme Court Voids Hutchison Concessions — Balboa & Cristobal Closed
description: Panama's Supreme Court voided Hutchison Port Holdings' concessions for the ports of Balboa and Cristobal. APM Terminals and Terminal Investment Limited (TIL) have taken interim control. During the transition period, there is NO entrance or exit of equipment and vessels at either port. Both are major transshipment hubs — Balboa on the Pacific side and Cristobal on the Atlantic/Caribbean side — so the regional impact on Latin American and Caribbean trade is severe. Vessels are diverting to alternative ports in Colombia (Cartagena, Buenaventura) and Mexico (Manzanillo, Lazaro Cardenas).
impacts: Complete cessation of vessel and equipment movement
Both Pacific and Atlantic Panama Canal entrances affected
Diversions to Colombian and Mexican ports adding 5-10 days
Panama Canal transits unaffected but feeder networks broken

title: Casablanca Port Congestion — 8.44 Days Average Waiting
description: Persistent congestion at Casablanca port with approximately 8.44 days average vessel waiting time. Driven by high import volumes, limited terminal capacity, and equipment availability issues at Morocco's primary container gateway.

title: Mombasa Port Congestion — Equipment Shortages
description: Mombasa port experiencing approximately 5.71 days average vessel waiting time due to equipment shortages and high transshipment volumes. Kenya's primary gateway for East African trade (Uganda, Rwanda, South Sudan, DRC) is bottlenecked.

title: Tema Port Congestion — Crane Outages
description: Tema port, Ghana, experiencing approximately 4.33 days average vessel waiting time with crane outages reducing lift capacity at West Africa's key hub.

title: Beira Port — Severe Systemic Congestion
description: Beira port, Mozambique, is experiencing severe systemic congestion rather than an active severe-weather closure. Latest operational indicators show high yard pressure, a fully occupied berth line, and double-digit vessel waiting times driven by equipment constraints, yard saturation, and ripple effects from earlier seasonal disruptions. Current weather context should not be treated as the primary cause unless Open-Meteo or direct port notices show severe conditions on the analysis date.
impacts: Double-digit vessel waiting times reported for Beira
Yard pressure around 85% fill in latest briefing
10 vessels at berth and 0 at anchorage indicates saturated berth and yard capacity
Equipment constraints and high yard utilization slowing cargo flow
Earlier seasonal disruptions may be contributing residual backlog, but current severe weather is not the active cause

title: Beira Corridor — EN6 Armed Attack Risk
description: Security risk remains elevated along the EN6 transport corridor linking Beira with Chimoio and inland markets. Armed attack risk on the corridor can affect truck reliability and inland clearance even when the port itself remains physically operational.
impacts: High geopolitical and corridor security risk
Armed attack risk along the EN6 Beira-Chimoio corridor
Potential trucking delays and convoy/security constraints inland
Inland logistics reliability reduced despite port-side operations continuing

title: Cape Town Wind-Bound Suspensions
description: Cape Town port experiencing wind-bound suspensions of terminal activity. The Cape of Storms lives up to its name with seasonal gale-force winds regularly halting container operations at the South African hub. This is especially impactful given increased Cape of Good Hope traffic from Hormuz rerouting.
impacts: Increased traffic from Hormuz rerouting compounds delays
Seasonal gale-force winds in South Atlantic

title: European Port Backlogs — Easter Holidays + Rail Congestion
description: Easter holiday backlogs across European ports combined with the ongoing Hamburg/northern rail corridor infrastructure congestion affecting connections to Bremerhaven, Wilhelmshaven, and Rotterdam. Labour strikes in Portugal and Greece earlier in the cycle affected berthing productivity. A worker strike in Italy has been reported affecting parcel transit times.
impacts: Bremerhaven and Wilhelmshaven rail connections strained
Portugal and Greece labour strikes affecting berthing
Italy worker strike affecting parcel transit
Barge and rail delays of 2-4 days

title: Ningbo Fog Season — Intermittent Closures
description: Ningbo-Zhoushan port hit by fog season causing intermittent port closures and increased vessel waiting times. As the world's busiest port by cargo tonnage, even short closures cascade through global schedules.
impacts: World's busiest port — cascading global schedule impact

title: Jakarta Port Congestion — 4.7 Days Waiting
description: Tanjung Priok (Jakarta) running heavily congested at approximately 4.7 days vessel waiting time. Indonesia's primary gateway is strained by high domestic demand and limited terminal expansion progress.

title: Surabaya Port Congestion — 4.9 Days Waiting
description: Surabaya port running heavily congested at approximately 4.9 days vessel waiting time. Indonesia's second-largest port strained by redirected volumes from Jakarta congestion and growing East Java industrial demand.

title: Chilean Ports — High-Swell Disruptions
description: High-swell conditions disrupting Chilean ports including San Antonio and Valparaíso. Pacific swell events making berthing hazardous and slowing cargo operations at Chile's primary container gateways.

title: New Zealand Ports — Severe Weather Challenges
description: Tauranga reporting approximately 2 days vessel waiting with severe weather causing berthing challenges. Lyttelton (Christchurch) running at approximately 3.5 days waiting. Seasonal autumn/winter storm patterns affecting both North and South Island ports.
impacts: North and South Island ports affected

title: Port of LA — Record Volumes Amid Tariff Uncertainty
description: Port of Los Angeles processed 890,861 TEU in April 2026, 5.7% above last year — the second-best April on record. However, ongoing tariff uncertainty is creating forward-booking volatility. Port leadership has flagged the Middle East war as the primary concern for supply chain stability. Front-loading of cargo ahead of potential tariff increases is contributing to elevated volumes.
impacts: 890,861 TEU in April — second-best April on record
Front-loading ahead of potential tariff increases
Middle East war flagged as primary supply chain risk

title: Cape of Good Hope Rerouting — Global Schedule Impact
description: Maersk and Hapag-Lloyd have rerouted Asia-Europe and Asia-US East Coast services around the Cape of Good Hope to avoid the Strait of Hormuz conflict zone. This adds approximately 4,000 nautical miles and 10-14 days to Asia-North Europe sailings, 7-10 days to Asia-Mediterranean, and 5-7 days to Asia-US East Coast via Suez. The additional capacity absorbed by longer voyages is effectively removing ~6-8% of global container fleet capacity from the market, supporting elevated freight rates.
impacts: 10-14 days added transit to North Europe
5-7 days added to US East Coast
~6-8% global fleet capacity absorbed by longer transits
Elevated freight rates across all major lanes
Australia-Europe transit times extended as vessels avoid Suez/Med routing

title: Australian Ports — MUA Protected Industrial Action
description: The Maritime Union of Australia (MUA) has commenced protected industrial action at DP World terminals across Australia including Melbourne, Sydney, Brisbane, and Fremantle. Work bans, 24-hour stoppages, and overtime restrictions are causing berthing delays of 2-4 days and yard congestion at Australia's major container gateways. The action follows failed enterprise bargaining negotiations since mid-2025, with the MUA seeking a 16% wage increase over two years.
impacts: 24-hour stoppages and work bans at DP World terminals
Berthing delays of 2-4 days at major Australian ports
Yard congestion building at Melbourne and Sydney
Impact compounded by Hormuz rerouting extending Europe-Australia transit
